schools.summary: Saul, sleepless with animosity and guided by the Ziphites, hunts David at En-gedi (Ain-jedy); the 'rocks of the wild goats' names a region thick with chamois.
Matthew Henry: home safe from the Philistines, Saul at once seeks David's ruin — unnatural malice, which Providence will soon deliver into David's power.
Clarke thinks only a small Philistine raid was repulsed; Wesley marvels that rage drove Saul to risk an army on those dangerous precipices.
Gill: Saul returned having driven the Philistines out of his country.
The three thousand chosen men show how far Saul feared David as a rival king; Keil locates the sheep-folds in the Wady Chareitun.
Wesley pictures the steep goat-rocks and a king so enraged he ventured his army among them.
Gill: the force was drawn from the army that had pursued the Philistines.

debates.question: Is 'the rocks of the wild goats' a description or a proper name?

sides.position: a descriptive term — 'ibex rocks,' where only wild goats find pasture
a proper name, as the Septuagint took it (Saddaiem)

schools.summary: Ellicott reads 'cover his feet' as a midday siesta, and stresses that from the dark cave David could see Saul while Saul saw nothing.
Clarke takes 'cover his feet' of relieving nature (with the ancient versions); Wesley, of sleep, God disposing Saul to it.
Gill: the sheepcotes were caves where flocks sheltered at noon.

debates.question: What does 'Saul went in to cover his feet' mean?

sides.position: to relieve nature / ease himself (Vulgate, LXX, Targum, Arabic)
to lie down and sleep a midday siesta (Peshito, Michaelis, Ewald)

outliers.note: Relates the rabbinic legend of a spider weaving her web over the cave's mouth, so that Saul entered without suspicion.

schools.summary: No word of Jehovah had come; the men mistook providence for an oracle (Keil), echoing real predictions of David's reign (Ellicott).
Clarke: David rose to take Saul's life, and his heart smote him for that intent; Wesley: the men construed the promises as an obligation.
Gill names David's chief officers, some reckoning Joab and Abishai, as the speakers.

debates.question: Did David 'arise' meaning to kill Saul, or only to cut the robe?

sides.position: he rose intending to kill, and was checked by conscience
the text stresses the men's urging and David's restraint, not a murderous purpose

schools.summary: Keil: his heart smote him because he counted even cutting the robe an injury to the king; Ellicott: rather for the momentary thought of slaying the Anointed.
Matthew Henry: a heart smiting us for small sins is a sign of a conscience awake and tender.
Gill: his conscience accused him and he repented of what he had done.

debates.question: Why did David's heart smite him?

sides.position: for the affront to the king's dignity in cutting the robe
not for the robe but for the momentary impulse to kill the Lord's Anointed

outliers.note: Records the Talmud's cryptic censure (Rabbi Yosi ben Chanina) on treating clothes slightingly, citing 1 Kings 1:1.

schools.summary: Clarke: Saul was appointed by God and removable only by God; to kill the Lord's anointed king would have made David a murderer.
Keil: the vehemence of the oath proves no word of Jehovah had licensed David to touch Saul.
Gill sets the saying in the action, whether spoken with the skirt in hand or in answer to the men.
Matthew Henry: David would not do the evil nor suffer others to do it, a type of Christ who saved his persecutors, overcoming evil with good.
Keil: David 'tore his men back' with words and did not let them rise to slay Saul, who then went out unharmed.
Clarke: since he could restrain them it was his duty; to connive would have made David the murderer.
Gill: David pacified and quieted his men by pleading that Saul was the anointed of the Lord.
David calls 'My lord king' with deepest reverence and profound homage, showing he is no rival king, yet with earnest words to prick Saul's conscience.
Matthew Henry: falsely charged, David shows providence gave the chance yet on a good principle he spared, leaving his cause to God.
Gill: David went out only after Saul had left, appealing at a safe distance yet in full view.
David's court enemies — Doeg, Cush the Benjamite (Psalm 7), the Ziphites — had poisoned Saul; David asks why the king heeds such men.
Matthew Henry: it is respect due to crowned heads to charge their faults on evil counsellors; behind them Satan the accuser keeps his agents.
Gill: David tactfully lays the blame on flattering courtiers rather than the king, to win a hearing.

themes: sparing the king even in speech

schools.summary: Saul's own eyes had seen his life in David's hand; Ellicott weighs the textual crux of who 'bade' the killing and of the supplied subject 'mine eye.'
Gill: this day Saul had full proof of David's forbearance, about to be shown in the skirt.

debates.question: Who 'bade' the killing in 'the LORD delivered thee... and some bade me kill thee'?

sides.position: 'some' / one of David's men urged it (English, Syriac, Chaldee)
literally 'the LORD bade,' or (Vulgate) 'I thought to kill thee'

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'my father' is reverence, not kinship, since Michal was likely already given to Phalti; Keil hears childlike affection in it.
Matthew Henry: David avows no evil in his hand and holds up the skirt — had he been guilty, Saul's head, not his hem, would be in David's hand.
Gill takes 'my father' both naturally (father-in-law) and civilly (a king should father his subjects).

debates.question: Does 'my father' mean father-in-law or reverent address?

sides.position: father-in-law (and king as father of his people)
simply the reverence of subject to sovereign; Michal already given away

schools.summary: Keil: having proved his innocence, David invokes the Lord as judge; 'evil proceeds from the evil,' so refusing revenge itself vindicates him.
Matthew Henry: David's fixed resolution never to be his own avenger; the justice of God is the refuge of oppressed innocence.
Gill: David will not be judge in his own cause but leaves it to God to determine.
Gill weighs 'the proverb of the ancient one' — Adam, or the Ancient of days — while all agree deeds reveal the doer.
Keil and Ellicott: only a wicked man avenges himself; actions answer to the quality of the mind ('from a bad raven, a bad egg').
Clarke and Wesley: had David been the conspirator alleged, he would not have spared so fair an opportunity to kill.

debates.question: Whose is 'the proverb of the ancients'?

sides.position: the ancient one — Adam, or the Ancient of days (the Lord)
simply an old maxim that wicked deeds betray a wicked heart

schools.summary: A dead dog cannot bite and a flea is beneath notice; David paints himself as harmless and negligible, 'one flea' being poor game for a royal hunter.
Matthew Henry: it is below a king to hunt a poor exiled shepherd; the eagle darts not upon flies, and it is enough for the lion to have laid his victim low.
Clarke: 'dead dog' and 'flea' are terms of sovereign contempt; the Targum renders both 'one weak, one contemptible.'
David calmly commits his cause to God to see the wrong and plead it, seeking not mere acquittal but an advocate and deliverer.
Gill: David disowns being judge in his own quarrel, leaving it to God's providence.
Clarke: let God determine who is guilty.

outliers.note: Crowns the verse with Bishop Sanderson's words: he will not touch the Lord's Anointed for a kingdom, but wait God's leisure.

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: Saul's tears are real yet not repentance — many mourn for sins they do not forsake, weeping while still in league with them.
David's conduct went to Saul's heart (Keil); Ellicott, with Philippson, paints the grandeur of the scene and calls the change transitory.
Gill: God's restraining providence and the skirt in David's hand awed Saul into listening.
Wesley: Saul knew the voice though not the face, and wept from the sense of his sin.
Gill: Saul owns David 'more righteous' yet still thinks himself righteous, confessing no true sense of his own sin.
Keil: David's conduct so went to Saul's heart that he owned he had rewarded evil for good.
Wesley: Saul ingenuously acknowledges both David's integrity and his own iniquity.

debates.question: How far does 'thou art more righteous than I' go?

sides.position: a comparison that stops short of contrition
an ingenuous owning of David's integrity and his own iniquity

schools.summary: Gill: the cutting of the skirt only, with David holding Saul's life, proved his good dealing beyond dispute.
Keil: Saul's own ground — 'when the Lord delivered me, thou killedst me not' — owns the mercy came at the height of David's power.
Matthew Henry: Saul recalls his mistaken suspicion; we too should be forward to recall suspicions once proved false.
Keil: the rhetorical question expects 'no' — men do not let enemies escape — so Saul prays the Lord to repay the good David has done.
Gill: it is not usual among men to let an enemy go unhurt, yet David did so.
Wesley: unable to recompense so great a favour, Saul turns the reward over to God.
Keil: David's conduct conquered the evil spirit for the moment, and Saul owned the conviction he could never suppress — that David would surely reign.
Gill: Saul knew it by providence, David's princely spirit, and Samuel's word; tradition links the cut skirt to the rent mantle of the kingdom.

debates.question: How did Saul 'know' David would be king?

sides.position: inferred from David's superior, kingly soul and magnanimity
chiefly from Samuel's prophecy and the known anointing

schools.summary: Ellicott: fearing the Eastern custom of exterminating a fallen royal house (Jeroboam, Baasha, Ahab), Saul begs an oath for his seed, as Jonathan had.
Clarke: Saul knew an oath would bind David, though his own oath to Jonathan had not bound himself.
Gill: David swore by the Lord; Keil notes he yet kept to the heights, distrusting the change.

themes: Saul's broken oaths versus David's word

schools.summary: Gill: David religiously kept the oath — sparing Mephibosheth, punishing Ishbosheth's murderers; the Gibeonite execution was God's own appointment.
Clarke and Wesley: Saul went home confounded but unchanged; David, taught by Saul's inconstancy, trusted him no more and kept to the hold.
Matthew Henry: it is dangerous to venture on a reconciled enemy's mercy; be innocent as doves yet wise as serpents.
Chrysostom (via Wordsworth): the cave became a church and David a bishop, offering the sacrifice of his own anger and gaining a glorious victory.

outliers.note: Allegorizes the cave as a Christian church and David as priest, sacrifice, and altar — his mastered anger the true offering.
